text: MSBE Irradiation Facility V-1 Neutron Flux.
MSBE Reactor Building Plan A-A 25
The general concept is based on studies of the 1000 Mw(e) single-fluid Molten Salt Breeder Reactor by E.
The design bases for the Molten Salt Breeder Experiment (MSBE) are based on information from the MSRE and the reference plant design of a 1000 Mw(e) single-fluid Molten Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR).
Calculations indicate that a 150 Mw(thermal) reactor is a reasonable size that would meet the project objectives for the MSBE.
The primary salt for the MSBE contains both the fissile $(^{233}\mathrm{U})$ and the fertile (Th) material.
The MSRE was a circulating molten-salt-fueled, graphite-moderated reactor that operated at 7.3 Mw(thermal) and a core outlet temperature of $1210^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The purpose of the MSRE was to provide a demonstration of the technology as it existed in the early 1960's and a facility for investigating the compatibility of fuels and materials, the chemistry of the fuels, and the engineering features of molten-salt reactors.

section: 2. THE MSRE AND THE PRESENT STATUS OF THE TECHNOLOGY
text: measurements made during operation of the reactor. $^{4}$ A description of the MSRE and a summary of the experience with it are given in Appendix I.

A conceptual design of a single-fluid 1000 Mw(e) Molten Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR) power station was made and is described in Appendix II and in more detail elsewhere.5,6 The fuel is contained in the primary salt which is a mixture of fluorides containing also the fertile material.

The reactor shall have the capability for exposing to a fast $(>50\mathrm{kev})$ neutron flux of $5\times 10^{14}$ neut. $\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$ sec $^{-1}$ in primary salt at temperatures up to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ core graphite elements that are of full MSBR cross section.
In this region of the core, the power density shall be above 500 w/cc of salt and the salt flow conditions shall be as close as practical to those proposed for the MSBR.
A conversion ratio near 1.0 is desirable but not essential.
In addition, the average power density of the circulating primary salt shall be near that of the MSBR (46 w/cc).

$\star \star$ Neutron flux, neutrons $\mathbf{cm}^{-2}$ sec-1 x 10-14 (unperturbed).
1, are located about one foot away from the core at a region where the fast flux ( $>0.1$ Mev) is less than $3 \times 10^{13}$ neut/cm² sec.

The fast flux ( $>0.1$ Mev) at the midplane of the vessel wall is less than $3 \times 10^{12}$ , giving the wall an integrated dose ( $>0.1$ Mev) of about $1 \times 10^{21}$ nvt in ten full power years.
The thermal flux at the vessel wall is only $2 \times 10^{10}$ neut/cm² sec.
This vessel opening is extended some 16 ft in height above the vessel midplane and some 8 ft above the salt level.
1 slide against a raised machined surface inside of the vessel extension just above the blanket region.
The primary salt has the following nominal composition (in mole $\%$ ): 71.5 ${}^{7}\mathrm{LiF}$ ; 16 $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ ; 12 $\mathrm{ThF}_4$ ; 0.5 $\mathrm{UF_4}$ .
(At the start in a system initially charged with $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ only, the $\mathrm{UF_4}$ would be 0.44 mole $\%$ ; at steady state, the total $\mathrm{UF_4}$ would be 0.57 mole $\%$ ).
The primary salt leaves a side outlet at the top of the reactor pressure vessel at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and enters the pump suction.
The flow is down through the tubes in the vertical heat exchanger, leaving at $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
For the layout studies, 10-inch pipe is shown between the components except at the pump suction where 12-inch pipe is used to reduce the velocity entering the pump.
The total primary salt flow through the core at 150 Mw(thermal) and with a $\Delta T$ of $250^{\circ}F$ is 6.37 x $10^{6}$ lb/hr (3850 gpm at $1300^{\circ}F$ ).
The velocity in the proposed 10-inch pipe is about 16 ft/sec.
This expansion tank is able to accommodate about $10\%$ of the primary system salt volume or about 30 ft³.
About $89\%$ of the primary salt flow goes through the reactor vessel, $10\%$ through the gas separation bypass, and the remainder to miscellaneous components such as the jet pump in the drain tank.
The total flow requirements at a $\Delta T$ of $250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is $4300\mathrm{gpm}$ .
However, it is proposed that a larger pump be used in the MSBE to permit operation with a $\Delta T$ of $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ at full power, if problems arise at the higher $\Delta T$ .
Therefore a pump with a minimum flow of $5400\mathrm{gpm}$ is proposed; the head required is 125 ft.
The pump would have a variable speed motor to permit operation at $4300\mathrm{gpm}$ .
4.4.1.2 Primary Heat Exchanger.
4 has the same type, size (3/8 in.
OD) and length (about 21 ft) of tubes as those proposed for the MSBR.
The velocity of the primary salt in the tubes is about 10 ft/sec and the pressure drop about 129 psi.

The overall heat transfer coefficient is estimated to be 950 Btu/hr·ft²·°F when a secondary salt composed of 92 mole % NaBF₄ and 8 mole % NaF with an average velocity of 7.5 ft/sec is used on the shell side.
4, is removable as a unit using the maintenance techniques proposed for removing the core graphite array.
As in the MSBR about $10\%$ of the primary salt flow leaving the pump is routed through a gas separator.

The afterheat in the primary system, twelve minutes after reactor shutdown, is about $2\%$ of the operating power.5 The afterheat in the fission gas from the primary system during operation is about $1\%$ of the reactor power.5 The primary drain tank heat removal system is designed for the higher of these two which is 3 Mw.
This coolant salt consists of 66 mole percent $^7\mathrm{LiF}$ and 34 mole percent $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ .
The coolant system is composed of ten independent loops, each of which is capable of removing $12\%$ of the total afterheat.
This jet is driven with a $40\mathrm{~gpm}$ side stream from the discharge side of the primary pump.
This jet is driven by an auxiliary pump of low capacity (around 300 gpm).

The storage tank utilizes a cooling system similar to that of the drain tank but of only one-tenth the capacity; therefore, the afterheat must be allowed to decay to 0.3 Mw before the salt is transferred to the storage tank.
An alternate approach for cooling of the storage tank is to use a system similar to that of the MSRE drain tank in which water was boiled in bayonet-type thimbles in the drain tank.<sup>10</sup>
At a power level of 150 Mw(thermal), the secondary salt enters the shell side of the primary heat exchanger at $850^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and leaves at $1150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The flow rate is $4.7 \times 10^{6} \, \text{lb/hr}$ or $5300 \, \text{gpm}$ at $1150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Feedwater is fed to the tubes at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 3800 psia.
Steam is generated with outlet conditions of $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 3600 psia.
A compensating reduction in pressure drop in the secondary system piping is achieved with a relatively low velocity of 13 ft/sec, which requires 12-inch pipe.
The feedwater conditions (700°F - 3800 psia) and the outlet steam conditions (1000°F - 3600 psia) are therefore the same in both reactors.
The secondary salt melting point is about $725^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The steam generators are fabricated from Hastelloy N, but all other components in the steam system can be conventional equipment made from material such as 2-1/4 Cr steel.
Heater thimbles penetrate the top of the cell and are capable of maintaining the interior at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The cell liner is about 26 ft in diameter and 34 ft in height.
5, and a plan of the cells within the building is shown in Fig.
The chemical processing flowsheet proposed for the MSBR<sup>11</sup> is given in Fig.
The processes are described in more detail elsewhere;<sup>11</sup> a brief description is given below.

Most of the bismuth stream leaving the extraction column is contacted with an $\mathrm{H_2}$ -HF mixture in the presence of about $10\%$ of the salt leaving the fluorinator in order to transfer materials such as uranium and protactinium to the salt stream.
Sufficient time is allowed for the decay of $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ so that the rate at which this material is lost is acceptably low.
The remaining materials, including Zr, Ni, $^{231}\mathrm{Pa}$ , and Pu, are withdrawn in the salt stream from the fluorinator.
The rare earths are then removed from the LiCl by contact with bismuth containing a high concentration of $^7$ Li.
Separate contactors are used for removal of the divalent and trivalent rare earths in order to minimize the quantity of $^7$ Li required.
Only about $2\%$ of the LiCl is fed to the contactor in which the divalent materials are removed.
8; some additional operating data were given previously in this report in Table I.
10 is a very detailed description of the reactor design.
The 54-in.-diameter MSRE core was comprised of bare graphite bars, 2-in. square and 64-in. long.
The graphite bars had a channel machined in each face to form $1.2 \times 0.4$ in. flow passages between bars.
The graphite (Grade CGB) was especially produced to limit pore size to 0.4 microns to keep out the salt (which does not wet the graphite).
A removable, 2-in.-diameter, Hastelloy N basket held specimens of metal and graphite in a special channel near the center of the core.
The fuel salt was circulated at 1200 gpm through the shell side of a U-tube heat exchanger, where heat was transferred to a secondary salt (LiF-BeF $_2$ , 66 - 34 mole %).
The coolant salt, circulating at 850 gpm, delivered the heat to an air-cooled heat exchanger.
The heat transfer capability of this system limited the power that could be dissipated to 7.3 Mw when the core operated at its normal temperature of 1210°F.
The pressure in the blanket gas (and at the open pump suction) was 5 psig.
Pump discharge pressures were 55 psig in the fuel loop and 70 psig in the coolant loop, respectively.

A tube into the top of the fuel pump bowl connected to the sampler-enricher, contained a motor-driven reel by which sample buckets or capsules of enriching salt $(\mathrm{LiF - UF_4}, 73 - 27 \text{ mole}\%)$ could be lowered into the salt pool.
A spray ring in the top of the fuel pump bowl took about 50 gpm of the pump discharge and sprayed it through the gas above the salt.
A flow of 3 liters/min of helium carried the xenon and krypton out of the pump bowl, through a holdup volume, a filter station, and a pressure-control valve to charcoal beds.
These consisted of pipes filled with charcoal, submerged in a water-filled pit at about $90^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Temperatures in the freeze valves in the fuel and coolant drain lines were controlled so they would thaw in 10 to 15 minutes when a drain was requested.
Water-cooled bayonet tubes extended down into thimbles in the drain tanks to remove up to 100 kw of heat, if necessary.
The cell atmosphere was kept at $140^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ by water-cooled, forced-air space coolers.
A fraction of the blower output was discharged past a radiation monitor and up the ventilation stack to keep the reactor and drain tank cells at -2 psig during operation.
A small bleed of nitrogen into the cell kept the oxygen content at $3\%$ to preclude fire if fuel-pump lubricating oil spilled on hot surfaces.
The 5-in. salt piping in the reactor cell included flanges that would permit removal of the fuel pump or the heat exchanger.
One whole batch of salt (about 75 ft³) could be transferred into the tank where it was sparged with hydrogen fluoride gas to remove H₂O and convert the metal oxides to fluorides.
Design of the MSRE was started in the summer of 1960.
The design, component development, procurement, and construction were conducted between this time and October 1964 when molten salt was first loaded in the MSRE.
At the time of the final shutdown in December 1969, salt had circulated in the fuel system for 21,788 hours, the reactor had been
critical for 17,655 hours and had produced heat equivalent to 13,172 full-power hours.
(A convenient review as of July 1969, appears in Ref.

Oxide contamination, which could lead to $\mathrm{UO_2}$ precipitation, was controlled by providing a blanket gas (ordinarily helium) from which oxygen and moisture had been removed by passage through a $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ titanium sponge.
Fuel salt analyses showed that the oxide level remained remarkably low ( $\sim 60$ ppm), well below any solubility limit.

There was some uncertainty in the valence state that several fission products would assume in the MSRE fuel salt environment, and it was recognized that if the average products of one fission reacted with more than $\frac{1}{4}$ fluorine atoms, some $\mathrm{UF}_4$ would be reduced to $\mathrm{UF}_3$ as a result of power operation.

It turned out that the reverse was true: the fission products tied up slightly less than $\frac{1}{4}$ fluorine atoms on the average, so $\mathrm{U}^{3+}$ was very gradually raised to $\mathrm{U}^{4+}$ .
corrosion), it was necessary only to make small ( $\sim 10 - g$ ) additions of beryllium metal through the sampler-enricher at intervals of a month or two.
Coulometric analysis of fuel salt samples for uranium showed good reproducibility and high precision $(\pm 0.5\%)$ and the indicated inventory changes agreed well with calculated burnup.
Tritium was produced in the MSRE fuel salt at a calculated rate of 40 Ci/day (35 Ci/day from $^6\mathrm{Li}$ and 5 Ci/day from $^7\mathrm{Li}$ ).
to about 25 Ci/day during sustained power operation.
Some tritium diffused through the heat exchanger tubes in the MSRE: 0.6 Ci/day was found in the coolant salt offgas and around 3 to 5 Ci/day appeared in the air going up the coolant stack.

The compatibility of the salt, the graphite moderator, and the Hastelloy N container material was demonstrated by analysis of hundreds of samples of fuel salt and examination of specimens exposed to salt in the core for as long as 14,714 hours.

(For a short time after the fuel processing and $233\mathrm{U}$ loading there was apparently some oxidation of iron also.) Analyses of the fuel salt showed a chromium increase over the 48 months from December 1965 to December 1969, equivalent to all the chromium in a 0.46-mil layer over the circulating loop surfaces.
Metallographic and microprobe examination of core specimens showed no void formation due to leaching, but there was a chromium concentration gradient from the surface to a depth of 2 to 3 mils.
Carburization of metal specimens in contact with graphite extended to a depth of only 1 mil.

The chromium concentration in the coolant salt remained at $37 \pm 7$ ppm through 26,076 hr of circulation and preliminary examination of radiator tube specimens shows no evidence of deposition by mass transport.
Graphite specimens exposed to fuel salt in the core for periods up to $14,714$ hr showed no attack; there were no changes in surface finish and no cracks other than those present before exposure.
(Assuming the specimens are typical, there is less than $10\mathrm{g}$ of uranium in the $3700\mathrm{kg}$ of graphite in the core.)

Specimens of the standard Hastelloy N heats used in constructing the MSRE, after exposure to thermal neutron fluences up to $1.5 \times 10^{21} \mathrm{n/cm}^2$ , showed drastically reduced fracture strain and creep rupture life at high temperatures.

(Fracture strains at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ were less than $1\%$ for some specimens.) Although these effects did not become limiting in the MSRE, they would make the standard alloy unsuited for high-flux, long-term applications.
Agreement of calculated and observed critical concentrations of fissile material was good, both in the $235\mathrm{U}$ and the $233\mathrm{U}$ startups.

From these measurements most accurate values were obtained for neutron yields $(\eta)$ for $235\mathrm{U}$ and $233\mathrm{U}$ in a neutron spectrum quite similar to that in molten salt breeder reactors.
The two original salt pumps served throughout the MSRE operation, accumulating 21,788 hr of salt circulation on the fuel pump and 26,076 hr on the coolant pump.
The oil, which leaked at roughly 5 cc/day into each pump, had no deleterious effect on the salt, but its thermal decomposition products accumulated at points in the offgas system.
This was overcome by installation of larger valves and a 3-stage filter.
After the third such operation in 24 months, a heater was installed so salt accumulations could be melted out.
The thermal rating of the plant is $2250\mathrm{Mw}$ .
The primary salt is circulated by four pumps operating in parallel and is heated from $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in its passage through the core.
Each pump has a capacity of about 16,000 gpm and circulates the salt through one of four primary heat exchangers and returns it to a common plenum at the bottom of the reactor vessel.

Each of the four secondary salt circuits has a pump of 20,000 gpm capacity which circulates the secondary salt from the corresponding primary heat exchanger through steam generators in adjacent cells.
The secondary salt is heated from $850^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to $1150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in a primary heat exchanger.
The steam generated is at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 3600 psia.
11 and a sectional elevation in Figs.
The reactor cell is normally maintained at a temperature of about $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ by electric heater thimbles to insure that the salts will be molten.
13, slopes to a drain line leading to the primary salt drain tank located in an adjacent cell.
The MSBR core design is based on replacement of the core graphite after an integrated neutron dose of about $3 \times 10^{22}$ neutrons/cm² (for E > 50 kev).
At a peak core power density of about 65 kw/liter, the anticipated core graphite life is about 4 years.
The calculated doubling time is 21 years.
The vessel is about 22 ft in diameter containing a graphite structure some 20 ft high.
For the proposed 70 psi inlet pressure, the Hastelloy N vessel would require an estimated 2 in. cylindrical thickness.
The fuel salt enters the bottom of the reactor vessel at $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , flows upward and leaves at the top at about $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The core Zone 1 is about 14 ft in diameter and consists of extruded graphite elements, 4 in. square x 13 ft long.

A minimum 0.6 in. diameter hole through the center of each element, and ridges oriented to separate the pieces, furnish flow passages and provide the requisite $13\%$ salt volume in this most active portion of the reactor.

Special shaped extensions on each end of the elements provide a greater salt-to-graphite volume ratio at the top and bottom of core Zone 1 to form an undermoderated region which helps reduce the axial neutron leakage from the core.
The maximum salt velocity in the core is about 8 ft/sec.
The overall pressure drop in the salt flowing through the core is about 26 psi.
Core Zone 2 consists of graphite slabs 2 in. thick and 13 ft long arranged radially around core Zone 1 to form a 17 ft diameter region.
The salt volume in core Zone 2 is about $37\%$ .
Graphite in the central region of core Zone 1 would require replacement after four years.
It was decided to replace all of the graphite in core Zones 1 and 2 as a unit.
Surrounding core Zone 2 in the radial direction is a 2 l/2 ft thick graphite reflector.
The reflector is composed of wedged-shaped slabs, about 1 ft wide at the thicker end and about 4 ft high.
These slabs are spaced about 1/4 in. from the vessel wall to allow an upward flow of fuel salt to cool the metal wall and maintain it below the design temperature of about $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
has a radial clearance of about $1\frac{1}{2}$ in. on the inside diameter to accommodate dimensional changes of the graphite and to allow clearances for removing and replacing the core assembly.
The amount of fuel salt in the radial and axial reflector regions is about $1\%$ of the reflector volume.
Each exchanger removes one-fourth of the 2250 Mw of thermal energy and has an estimated 5900 tubes, 3/8 in.
OD and about 21 l/2 ft long.
The velocity in the tubes is 10 ft/sec; the velocity of the secondary salt is typically 7.5 ft/sec.
The overall heat transfer coefficient is estimated to be 950 Btu/hr-ft $^2$ -°F.
This will be accomplished in a $10\%$ bypass flow around the heat exchanger and core.
After a minimum holdup of one hour, the gas is sent to charcoal beds for 135 Xe holdup and decay.
To reduce the possibility of freezing of the secondary salt, the feedwater enters the steam generators at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Supercritical steam is generated at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 3600 psia.
There are four 140 Mw thermal steam generators in each of the four secondary systems.
Part of the steam from the steam generators will be used to heat the feedwater in a mixing tee from about $550^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to the $700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ required.
Robertson, MSRE Design and Operations Report, Part I, Description of Reactor Design, ORNL-TM-728, January 1965, Oak Ridge National Laboratory.
